2003-03-01.title: Russia Orders a New Trial In Chechnya Murder Case
2003-03-01.abstract: Russian Supreme Court, in victory for family of slain woman Elza Kungayeva, rejects verdict in murder trial of prominent army Col Yuri Budanov and rules that military court ignored procedure; Budanov, decorated army commander, had admitted strangling 18-year-old Chechen woman after abducting her in March 2000 but claims he killed her in fit of rage; military court had declared that Budanov was temporarily insane at time of killing; he will stand trial again before new judge (M)

2003-03-01.title: Pakistani Guards Shot Dead at U.S. Office
2003-03-01.abstract: Two Pakistani police officers are killed and five others and passerby are wounded when at least one gunman attacks officers guarding American Consulate in Karachi; Afghan believed to have ties to militant Pakistani group is captured; no Americans are harmed in shootout; photos (M)

2003-03-01.title: Milan's new deal:Smells like team spirit
2003-03-01.abstract: MILAN—  The iconic Roman couturier Robert Capucci, silver-haired, sitting ramrod straight in a formal suit, watched Friday as the first of a trio of stylists interpreted his name. After a parade of soft, slouchy clothes with references to Capucci's boxy sculptures in their folds, out came the Belgian designer Bernhard Willhelm in hooded sweat top. His capsule collection was applauded by the Spanish Sybilla (who is the designer next in line along with the American Tara Subkoff).

2003-03-01.title: U.S. Lists 3 Chechen Groups As Terrorist and Freezes Assets
2003-03-01.abstract: US State Department spokesman Richard Boucher announces designation of three rebel groups in Chechnya--Riyadus-Salikhin Reconnaissance and Sabotage Battalion of Chechnen Martyrs, Special Purpose Islamic Regiment and Islamic International Brigade--as 'terrorist organizations' linked to Al Qaeda and freezes their assets in US; Russian Foreign Min Igor Ivanov welcomes decision (M)

2003-03-01.title: Former Prime Minister Elected President in Czech Republic
2003-03-01.abstract: Vaclav Klaus, forced to resign as prime minister in 1998 amid scandal over party financing, is elected during parliamentary session to replace old rival Pres Vaclav Havel; some experts predict that Klaus, right-wing free marketeer, could become more involved in day-to-day political fray than Havel, who has retired; photo (M)

2003-03-02.title: To White House, Inspector Is Now More a Dead End Than a Guidepost
2003-03-02.abstract: Relations between Hans Blix, United Nations chief weapons inspector for chemical and biological arms, and Bush administration grow more strained as Blix mixes his criticism of Iraq's cooperation with statements about progress; administration officials believe Blix is now more interested in pleasing all sides than stating face, and some are coming to regard inspections as sideshow to real issues of whether US can get necessary votes from divided Security Council to winapproval of resolution implicitly authorizing force against Iraq; photo (M)

2003-03-02.title: Unarmed American Intruder Roils Britain
2003-03-02.abstract: The American ruddy duck has been flying around Britain for 50 years but only now is showing its capacity to hit the British where they live.

2003-03-02.title: Once Secure, Argentines Now Lack Food and Hope
2003-03-02.abstract: Poverty and hunger are on rise in Argentina, country that was largely middle class just a few years ago and that still has more cattle than people; situation appears more accute in Tucuman and elsewhere in country's northwest and especially among the most vulnerable: the very young and very old; crumbling of government services shifts burden of caring for those in need increasingly on prviate charities; photos; map; graphs (M)

2003-03-02.title: Ireland Faces Harsh Reality About Abuse Of Alcohol
2003-03-02.abstract: Alcohol-related social problems are becoming more visible in Ireland, which until decade ago trailed other European countries in per capital alcohol consumption; Irish companies collectively lose more than $1 billion a year because of hung-over employees calling in sick; side effects like car accidents and medical care cost Irish economy another $1.5 billion; government will introduce legislation to require bars to check indentification for people 18 to 25, and some politicians suggest ban on drinking at all government functions; this and other efforts to address problem are hampered by social tolerance for alcohol abuse and perception that drinking is necessary part of being Irish (M)

2003-03-02.title: Iraq Begins Destroying Missiles U.N. Ruled Illegal
2003-03-02.abstract: Demetrius Perricos, deputy United Nations weapons inspector, says Iraq has begun destroying its illegal short-range Al Samoud 2 missiles, crushing 4 of stock of about 100; says it will take two weeks for Iraq to destroy the rest, along with their warheads, 380 illegally imported engines, any fuel and all design software; says host of other questions about Iraqi cooperation with his team have yet to be resolved; suggests that inspections can still be effective; photo (M)

2003-03-03.title: An Iranian-Backed Brigade, With Ties to the Kurds, Sets Up Camp in Northern Iraq
2003-03-03.abstract: Local Kurdish officials say advanced elements of Badr Brigade, Iranian-backed militia that includes many deserters from Iraq's army, are building new military camp in northern Iraq and preparing to move several thousand fighters into area; expanding activities of brigade, which intelligence officials say might fight as Iranian proxy against Pres Saddam Hussein, pose diplomatic challenges to both Kurdish authorities and United States; camp is 11 miles inside Iraq in Kurdish-controlled area; camp officials say brigade would join with Kurds to resist Turkish incursion; Bush administration has tried to limit regional intervention in event of war with Iraq; map; photo (M)

2003-03-03.title: 6 Gulf States Fail to Back Proposal Urging Hussein to Quit
2003-03-03.abstract: Arab leaders are debating whether to call on the Iraqi leader Saddam Hussein to step aside, but are deeply divided.

2003-03-03.title: Votkinsk Journal; So It Wheezes. It Was Still Tchaikovsky's Piano.
2003-03-03.abstract: Peter Ilyich Tchaikovsky's boyhood piano, instrument of first serious compositions of perhaps Russia's greatest composer, is on display in museum in Votinsk and is in urgent need of restoration; piano is 150 years old and has never been renovated because of lack of money; map; photo (M)

2003-03-03.title: Unlike U.S., France wields its veto power sparingly
2003-03-03.abstract: PARIS:—  In the 57-year history of the United Nations, the five permanent members of the UN Security Council have vetoed more than 250 proposals, but seldom has the power to say no raised as much political dust as France's possible use of its veto to block authorization of the use of force in Iraq.

2003-03-03.title: Rising czars of Russia's retail luxury
2003-03-03.abstract: MILAN—  None of the buzz at Prada's show last Friday included the thirty-something man in the dark suit and sunshine yellow tie sitting front row. Yet Leonid Friedland is one of the most powerful men in fashion.

2003-03-04.title: Greek Terror Defendants Don't Look the Part
2003-03-04.abstract: ''You're the beekeeper?'' a judge asked Dimitris Koufodinas, trying to distinguish him from the other defendants in a maximum-security courtroom here.    Mr. Koufodinas, 45, nodded. Until last summer, he had been living in this city's suburbs, tending hives on a grant from the European Union. Then the police began looking for him, and he hid for weeks in a tent at a nudist beach before turning himself in.

2003-03-04.title: Chirac Sees New Day for France and Algeria
2003-03-04.abstract: Evoking ''the peace of the brave'' of de Gaulle, President Jacques Chirac of France invited Algerians today to face the two countries' shared history and move on.     In a speech to the Algerian Parliament, Mr. Chirac spoke of ''shattered destinies and dreams'' and families torn apart in Algeria's bitter eight-year war of independence, which ended with Algeria throwing off French rule in 1962 and which left more than a million people dead.

2003-03-04.title: U.S. Troops' Chemical Suits Do Not Leak, Army Insists
2003-03-04.abstract: The Army's top officers for combating chemical and biological weapons said today that American ground forces arrayed near Iraq had been issued the newest generation of protective suits, and not older ones, many of which had been found defective.    Each Army soldier facing Iraqi forces has been issued two of the new protective uniforms and each marine has been issued three, the Army officers said.

2003-03-04.title: World Briefing | Europe: Estonia: Leftists Reeling After Election
2003-03-04.abstract: Edgar Savisaar, Center Party leader and Tallinn mayor, expresses confidence that he will become next Estonian Prime Minister despite split in elections giving both Res Publica Party and Center Party 28 seats in 101-seat parliament (S)

2003-03-04.title: British and Irish Leaders Seek Progress on Belfast Pact
2003-03-04.abstract: Prime Ministers Tony Blair of Britain and Bertie Ahern of Ireland held a series of crisis meetings today with Northern Ireland's main political parties for the second time in a month, trying to resolve a deadlock in carrying out the five-year-old Belfast peace accord.    Politicians from all sides described the talks as the most important negotiations since 1998, when the landmark agreement established a local government for the British province that shared power between Protestants and Roman Catholics.

2003-03-05.title: U.S. Reassures Seoul After Deploying Bombers
2003-03-05.abstract: South Korean officials said today that they were reassured by U.S. pledges not to stage a surprise attack against North Korea.

2003-03-05.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Troops To Leave Chechnya
2003-03-05.abstract: Russia Defense Ministry plans to remove 1,270 troops and 200 pieces of military equipment from Chechnya as soon as part of program to reduce military checkpoints and other evidence that province is effectively under military occupation (S)

2003-03-05.title: CHURCH DELEGATION IN RUSSIA
2003-03-05.abstract: Delegation of New York-based National Council of Churches meets with Russian officials as part of effort by council to spread its message against war with Iraq (S)

2003-03-05.title: U.S. Sending 2 Dozen Bombers In Easy Range of North Koreans
2003-03-05.abstract: Senior Pentagon officials say two dozen long-range bombers will be sent to Guam for possible use against North Korea if diplomatic efforts by Pres Bush fail to prevent North Korea from making nuclear weapons; describe deployment as preventive measure, aimed at maintaining strong deterrent force in Pacific; Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld reportedly signed order for deployment of B-52 and B-1 bombers even before North Korean MIG fighters intercepted and tailed unarmed US surveillance plane over international waters; White House officials play down deployment; describe it as insurance against North Korean 'opportumism' if military action begins in Iraq; note Pres Bush still believes diplomacy will eventually prevail in restraining North's nuclear program; photo; maps (M)

2003-03-05.title: Joint Declaration on Iraq
2003-03-05.abstract: Following is the official text of the joint declaration made by the foreign ministers of France, Russia and Germany today setting out their position on Iraq, as provided by Reuters:.

2003-03-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Trial Starts For Accused 'Carlos' Aide
2003-03-06.abstract: Johannes Weinrich, German man believed to be top associate of convicted Venezuelan terrorist Ilich Ramirez Sanchez, known as Carlos, goes on trial in Berlin on 6 counts of murder and 153 counts of attempted murder in what prosecutors say were terrorist activities across Europe in 1970's and 80's; is already serving life sentence for 1983 attack in West Berlin (S)

2003-03-06.title: 11 Palestinians Killed in Gaza Raid
2003-03-06.abstract: Israeli forces stormed a Gaza Strip refugee camp today, pursuing a Hamas leader after a suicide bomber killed 15 people.

2003-03-06.title: A Deepening Fissure
2003-03-06.abstract: The declaration issued by Europe's largest powers against war in Iraq deepened fissures with major U.S. allies.

2003-03-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Hospitals Cover Up Waiting Problems
2003-03-06.abstract: British government audit commission finds that more than half National Health Service hospitals have incorrectly reported waiting lists and some have deliberately doctored figures; Prime Min Tony Blair has promised to reduce delays in receiving treatment as part of his government's $64 billion health service reform (S)

2003-03-06.title: World Briefing | Americas: Argentina: President Seeks Election Monitors
2003-03-06.abstract: Argentine Pres Eduardo Duhalde asks Orgn of American States to send monitors to assure 'greater transparency' in presidential election scheduled for April 27 (S)

2003-03-07.title: Powell's Remarks to Security Council
2003-03-07.abstract: Following is the transcript of Secretary of State Colin L. Powell's statement today to the Security Council as recorded by the Federal News Service.

2003-03-07.title: U.S.-British Draft Resolution Stating Position on Iraq
2003-03-07.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, March 7 — Following is the revised text of a draft United Nations Security Council resolution on Iraq presented to the Council today. It was proposed by Britain and co-sponsored by the United States and Spain. The new text would give Saddam Hussein until March 17 to disarm or face the possibility of war. It is identical to the draft introduced on Feb. 14 except for the last four operative paragraphs. THE SECURITY COUNCIL,

2003-03-07.title: Israel Takes Back Land in Gaza; Raids Kill 3 and Wound 15
2003-03-07.abstract: After weeks of intensified raids into Gaza, Israel for the first time has begun doing here what it has done since last summer in the West Bank.

2003-03-07.title: U.N. Split Widens as Allies Dismiss Deadline on Iraq
2003-03-07.abstract: The U.S. and Britain will push the U.N. to vote on a new resolution that would give Iraq until March 17 to disarm. France, Russia and China dismissed the proposal.

2003-03-07.title: Israelis Storm a Gaza Camp; 11 Palestinians Are Killed
2003-03-07.abstract: Hours after Palestinian suicide bomber kills 15 people aboard bus in Haifa, armored Israeli forces storm Jabiliya refugee camp in Gaza Strip in pursuit of Hamas leader; 11 Palestinians are killed in explosion at camp's edge as Israeli forces withdraw; Palestinian witnesses claim Israeli tank fired shell into crowd, but Israeli Army rejects that account, claiming casualties were caused by Palestinian explosive; three crude rockets are fired from Gaza at Israeli town of Sederot, without injury; shortly after, Israeli tanks moved back into northern Gaza Strip for what has makings of extended stay; photo (M)

2003-03-08.title: White House Criticism Draws Sharp Rebuttal By Republican
2003-03-08.abstract: House Appropriations Committee chairman C W Bill Young, in letter to White House chief of staff Andrew Card, disputes assertions by Pres Bush and other administration officials that Congress shortchanged domestic security by not fulfilling request for $3.5 billion in antiterror funding for local emergency crews; says final bill provides $3.465 billion to support first responders through allocations to Federal Emergency Management Agency and Justice Department grant program (M)

2003-03-08.title: Keep troops in place, Seoul tells the U.S.
2003-03-08.abstract: SEOUL:—  Alarmed South Korean officials said Friday that Washington had ignored them in suggesting a realignment of U.S. forces in Korea and demanded that American troops stay where they are at least until resolution of the North Korean nuclear issue.

2003-03-08.title: Troop Movement Alone Could Cost $25 Billion, Congressional Office Finds
2003-03-08.abstract: Congressional Budget Office estimates that initial cost of deploying troops and equipment to Persian Gulf to fight Iraq and returning them home would cost nearly $25 billion; estimates that cost of first month of combat would be $10 billion and each additional month would fall slightly, to about $8 billion; says total cost of potential war would depend on how long hostilities last and how much is spent on reconstruction and other assistance (M)

2003-03-08.title: Senate Republicans Back Bush's Iraq Policy, as Democrats Call It Rash and Bullying
2003-03-08.abstract: Majority leader Bill Frist and other Senate Republicans, rallying behind Pres Bush, say they fully support his intent to disarm Saddam Hussein with or without UN backing if Hussein does not do so on his own; Senators Christopher Dodd and Edward Kennedy and other Senate Democrats challenge Republican view, contending UN weapons inspectors are producing results that could peacefully resolve crisis; photos (M)

2003-03-08.title: Fierce, Stinging Winds Destroy 17 Army Tents in Kuwait
2003-03-08.abstract: Fierce, stinging wind, with gusts of over 70 miles an hour, destroys 17 tents, including mess tent, in US military camp outside Kuwait City; hundreds of troops rush outdoors to hold down tents during worst of storm; senior military officials, noting that sandstorms are common from mid-February to mid-April, says crucial element in invasion of Iraq would be air assaults led by helicopters; photos (M)

2003-03-09.title: As Peace Accord Is Fleshed Out, Ivory Coast Fighting Resumes
2003-03-09.abstract: Fighting breaks out in western Ivory Coast, with civilians fleeing their ransacked villages and men firing at French soldiers who are enforcing cease-fire; officials representing Ivoirian government of Pres Laurent Gbagbo and rebels meet in Accra, Ghana, and agree to composition of national reconciliation government; rebels drop demand for control of Interior and Defense Ministries in exchange for seats on what is to be national security council, which will have authority over all matters relating to country's defense (M)

2003-03-09.title: Israel Kills a Top Hamas Leader; Arafat Promotes Critic of Uprising
2003-03-09.abstract: Israel kills Ibrahim al-Makadmah, top leader of Hamas, and his three bodyguards in Gaza City, just before Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat nominates critic of Palestinians' armed uprising to new post of prime minister; Hamas vows to avenge killing, calling Makadmah a purely political leader; Israeli security officials call him central figure in planning, approval and executing of terrorist operations of Hamas; newly named Palestinian prime minister, Mahmoud Abbas, is deputy in Palestine Liberation Orgn; his appointment is step toward meeting demands for change from Palestinian reformers as well as Bush administration and Israel; at issue is how much power Arafat would cede to him; day's events reflect two fronts of conflict--new rush of violence on one hand and halting search for diplomatic avenues on other; Israel takes rare step of permitting some Palestinian leaders to cross army checkpoints to reach Ramallah for Arafat's annoucement of Abbas's appointment; photos; map (M)

2003-03-09.title: Baghdad Destroys Missiles, Demands Concessions and Exploits Allies' Rift
2003-03-09.abstract: Overview of day's developments in Iraq crisis (M)

2003-03-09.title: C.I.A. Warning Of Terror Risk To G.I.'s in Iraq
2003-03-09.abstract: CIA warns that terrorists based in Iraq are planning attacks against American and allied forces inside country after any invasion; assessment goes beyond threat posed by Saddam Hussein's military forces; predicts for first time that groups inside Iraq affiliated with Al Qaeda may become engaged in war, even if Iraq's military is defeated and government overthrown; CIA conclusions are based on recently collected intelligence in form of intercepted communications of four to eight midlevel Qaeda operatives said to have been spotted in Iraq; presenting evidence to prove direct connection between Al Qaeda and Iraq has been one of most contentious aspects of Bush administration's effort to make its case for disarming Hussein of his weapons of mass destruction; CIA disclosure could strenegth admininstration's case; photo (M)

2003-03-09.title: Uncertainty of War Unsettles Oil Industry and the Economy
2003-03-09.abstract: Uncertainty about whether there will be war in Iraq and how war might affect oil production and prices makes planning hard for oil producers, consumers, businesses and investors; some traders are positioning themselves for prices to plunge if war starts, but most analysts say key indicators of oil industry's health -- notably low inventories of oil and petroleum products at American refineries -- suggest that prices will remain steep regardless of military action; graph of oil prices since 1990 (M)

2003-03-10.title: The Collections / Paris : A stellar McQueen; elegance at Viktor & Rolf
2003-03-10.abstract: PARIS—  Why is Paris different from any other fashion capital? The answer was blowing in the mighty wind that buffeted the model, kimono streaming behind her like cloud vapor, as she crossed the walkway at Alexander McQueen's show.

2003-03-10.title: Briefly Noted
2003-03-10.abstract: ARAB OFFICIALS TO VISIT IRAQ A high-level Arab delegation will travel to Baghdad in the coming days to meet Iraqi officials and try to avert an American-led war, Egypt's foreign minister, Ahmed Maher, said. Mr. Maher said the delegation, formed after the Arab summit meeting in Egypt earlier in March, would be

2003-03-10.title: Workers' Plight Brings New Militancy in China
2003-03-10.abstract: Ailing state-owned factories in Jiamusi, China, have laid off huge numbers of workers in last five years, leaving families with nothing to live on; disgruntled workers there have been staging series of protests since fall; such protests have spread across China, forcing government to act; protests are technically illegal, but workers' grievances are often seen as legitimate and their plight evokes enormous public sympahty; police are being trained to manage rather than crush protests; local officials now tend to meet with protesters, acceding to at least some of their monetary demands; workers see larger protests leading to larger solutions, but fear to test limits of police tolerance; map; photo (M)

2003-03-10.title: Republic of Malta
2003-03-10.abstract: MODERN HISTORY Britain took possession of the archipelago in 1814 and lost it in 1964, when Malta gained its independence. It became a republic in 1974. During World War II, Malta suffered devastating air raids by German and Italian forces, and the entire population was awarded the George Cross for bravery.

2003-03-10.title: Iraq Tries to Cover Its Bases, Both Diplomatic and Martial
2003-03-10.abstract: Iraq prepares for war while also trying to bolster case of nations opposed to invading Iraq; Lt Gen Hussam Muhammad Amin downplays signficance of proposed Mar 17 disarmament deadline, news conference; dismisses as technical details chief United Nations arms inspector Hans Blix's demands that Iraq prove what it did with specific quantities of lethal substances (M)

2003-03-11.title: Q&A / Joel Mornet : Big challenge for Parisian store
2003-03-11.abstract: Joel Mornet is the managing director of the Galeries Lafayette department store chain where ready-to-wear, sportswear and accessories account for nearly 70 percent of the flagship's annual turnover, and French fashion represents 65 percent of the total women's fashion on offer. Mornet spoke with Katherine Weisman about the challenges facing a Parisian department store.

2003-03-11.title: Allied Plan Would Encourage Iraqis Not to Fight
2003-03-11.abstract: American and British commanders say they are devising a strategy intended to defeat the Iraqi military without completely destroying it, and to limit damage to Iraq's infrastructure.    Lt. Gen. David McKiernan, the commander of allied land forces if the United States leads an invasion of Iraq, said he and other commanders were working out procedures for Iraqi units to signal their intention to stay out of the war.

2003-03-11.title: Annan Says U.S. Will Violate Charter if It Acts Without Approval
2003-03-11.abstract: Secretary General Kofi Annan warned today that if the United States fails to win approval from the Security Council for an attack on Iraq, Washington's decision to act alone or outside the Council would violate the United Nations charter.     ''The members of the Security Council now face a great choice,'' Mr. Annan said in The Hague, where he was trying to broker a United Nations deal on Cyprus. ''If they fail to agree on a common position and action is taken without the authority of the Security Council, the legitimacy and support for any such action will be seriously impaired.''

2003-03-11.title: 'No matter what,' Chirac says, as Bush presses for support : France promises 'no' vote
2003-03-11.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  President George W. Bush undertook a furious round of telephone diplomacy Monday in an attempt to salvage support for a UN resolution to authorize force against Iraq, but France said it was prepared to do whatever was necessary to block it.

2003-03-11.title: Brand survival:Is it better to merge?
2003-03-11.abstract: MILAN—  To merge or not to merge, that is still a difficult question for successful fashion designers looking to survive in ferociously competitive markets. The industry is still consolidating and looking to focus increasingly on the most lucrative parts of the market where business nous is nearly as important as design.

2003-03-12.title: The Collections / PARIS : At colorful YSL, Ford visits the bordello
2003-03-12.abstract: PARIS—  The show tent was, as ever, as dark and rich as a box of black chocolates. But ribbons glowed violet, vermilion, aquamarine, baby blue, molten marmalade. For his Saint Laurent Rive Gauche show, Tom Ford, fashion prince of darkness, had discovered color.

2003-03-12.title: European Union in New Warning on Bush Go-It-Alone War
2003-03-12.abstract: The foreign relations head of the E.U. warned today that Europe might withhold money for the reconstruction of Iraq if the U.S. waged war without the U.N. approval.

2003-03-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Shades Of Francis Gary Powers
2003-03-12.abstract: Russia's air force commander reacts to flight path of US U-2 spy planes near Russian border; flights conducted in effort to help Georgia's military forced Russian aircraft into manuever reminiscent of 1960 incident involving Francis Gary Powers; US air force says U-2 never came closer than 12.5 miles to Russia (S)

2003-03-12.title: Arab Nations Allow Antiwar Protests on Carefully Controlled Terms
2003-03-12.abstract: Arab leaders are more accustomed to muzzling public opinion than managing it, but the looming war against Iraq has presented them with a rare and risky opportunity to show solidarity with their people.    Over the last two weeks, governments in several Arab countries have permitted antiwar demonstrations and refrained from their normal practice of arresting protesters or smothering their voices in a sea of police officers.

2003-03-12.title: A Postponed Deadline, a Beleaguered Blair and De-Frenched Fries
2003-03-12.abstract: The United States budged slightly at the United Nations on its ultimatum for Iraq, where the mood was growing tense in expectation of war. Hairline fractures appeared in the relationship between the United States and Britain, and new potential troubles with Turkish cooperation in military planning emerged.    A MODEST RETREAT -- After opposition to Washington's war plan from France and Russia grew more categorical, the Bush administration backed off today from its deadline of Monday for United Nations weapons inspections. But President Bush wants a vote on a resolution by the end of the week giving Saddam Hussein an ultimatum to disarm or face attack, the White House said.

2003-03-13.title: U.S. Plans to Renew North Korea Spy Flights
2003-03-13.abstract: The U.S. planned today to resume spying on North Korean military activities from the skies above the Sea of Japan.

2003-03-13.title: Killings Shaped Serbia And Also Roiled Europe
2003-03-13.abstract: Assassinations like killing of Prime Min Zoran Djindjic have profoundly shaped Serbia's political life and at times thrown Europe into conflict and turmoil (M)

2003-03-13.title: Serb's Killing Is Setback for War Crimes Tribunal
2003-03-13.abstract: Killing of Prime Min Zoran Djindjic of Serbia is seen as setback at United Nations tribunal appointed to try crimes of Yugoslavia's wars; prosecutors there regarded him as their best hope to deliver important Serbian fugitives indicted by court; when Slobodan Milosevic was handed over to tribunal, Djindjic shunted aside objections of other senior Serbian politicians (M)

2003-03-13.title: 2 U.S. Officers Expect More North Korean 'Provocations'
2003-03-13.abstract: Two most senior officers overseeing American military operations in South Korea say it is likely that North Korea will step up its provocations in bid to gain financial aid and diplomatic attention from United States; Adm Thomas B Fargo and Gen Leon J LaPorte say North Korea would perhaps start up its nuclear reprocessor, provoke incident along demilitarized zone or at sea, or even conduct underground nuclear test; photo (M)

2003-03-13.title: Report Says Plan to Safeguard Nuclear Material Is Lacking
2003-03-13.abstract: Report issued by Harvard Univ researchers states that United States lacks comprehensive plan for protecting world's supply of nuclear material from terrorists; says nations of former Soviet Union present biggest risk, given their vast supply of nuclear material and deteriorating financial state; report is part of three-year research project commissioned by Nuclear Threat Initiative, Washington foundation, to create report card on nuclear security around world (M)

2003-03-14.title: Sandstorms come early to Kuwaiti desert this year
2003-03-14.abstract: KUWAIT:—  An unusually harsh sandstorm has swept through Kuwait, disrupting air traffic and exposing thousands of American, British and Kuwaiti troops to blinding desert winds for several hours.

2003-03-14.title: World Briefing | Europe: Northern Ireland: Bomb-Packed Van In Belfast
2003-03-14.abstract: British Army bomb squad performs controlled detonation of van outside Langanside Courthouse, Belfast; vehicle, loaded with three timed pipe-bombs and 26 gallons of gasoline, is suspected work of dissident republican groups opposing IRA involvement in peace initiative; incident is timed to draw attention away from meeting between Northern Ireland leaders and President Bush (S)

2003-03-14.title: Bush Statement on Middle East
2003-03-14.abstract: Following is the text of a statement by President George W. Bush Friday morning at the White House, as recorded by The New York Times:.

2003-03-14.title: U.S. MAY ABANDON U.N. VOTE ON IRAQ, POWELL TESTIFIES
2003-03-14.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L Powell says United States might choose to abandon second United Nations resolution authorizing military action against Iraq, week after Pres Bush vowed to force countries to vote on issue; Powell's comment, along with White House statements, seen as evidence that White House is losing hope of winning majority vote on resolution and may proceed to war without one; until now Bush has insisted on vote, but Powell reportedly argued that it would be politically less damaging to go to war without UN vote than to proceed under shadow of explicit rejection of military force; preparations for war are speeding forward; Turkey dismisses third American appeal--this time from Bush and Vice Pres Cheney--to use its territory as base for strikes against Iraq; White House pessimism over Security Council votes is voiced by Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain, who is quoted as saying diplomatic solution is 'probably less likely' and prospect of military action 'more likely' than at any time; both American and British officials blame France for bringing world closer to war, by its immediate rejection of Britain's proposal for Iraq to meet six specific benchmarks to demonstrate it is truly disarming; photos (M)

2003-03-14.title: Powell at New Turning Point in His Evolution on Iraq War
2003-03-14.abstract: With the Bush administration's diplomatic efforts on Iraq seemingly on the verge of collapse, this is an excruciating moment for Secretary of State Colin L. Powell.

2003-03-15.title: A Lifelong Obsession With Women, in Pictures
2003-03-15.abstract: Profile of Japanese photographer Nobuyoshi Araki, whose favorite subject, by far, is the female nude; Araki, during interview in city's Shinjuku district, comments on his obsession with women; photo (M)

2003-03-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Guatemala: Panel To Look At Attacks
2003-03-15.abstract: Guatemalan government agrees to establish commission, presided over by representatives from UN, Organization of American States and government, to investigate clandestine groups believed responsible for violence against human rights advocates (S)

2003-03-15.title: Bush Promises To Adopt Plan For the Mideast
2003-03-15.abstract: Pres George Bush, following several days of negotiations with British Prime Min Tony Blair, promises to adopt long-deferred peace plan for Palestinian state as soon as Palestinians choose new prime minister; says, during statement in White House Rose Garden, 'there can be no peace for either side in the Middle East unless there is freedom for both;' Blair, moments later, hails Bush's announcement as showing 'obligation of even-handedness' on eve of possible war against Iraq; photo (M)

2003-03-15.title: In Ceremony With No Surprises, China Formally Changes Leaders
2003-03-15.abstract: China's legislature, voting 2,937 to 4 with 3 abstensions, completes leadership transition by replacing Pres Jiang Zemin with Hu Jintao; re-elects Jiang as head of Central Military Commission and longtime protege Zeng Qinghong as deputy president (M)

2003-03-15.title: Mysterious Respiratory Illness Afflicts Hundreds Globally
2003-03-15.abstract: World Health Organization, issuing its first global alert in decade, reports hundreds of people in Vietnam, Hong Kong and China, mainly hospital workers, have developed mysterious respiratory illness that has killed at least six people and left most others with severe breathing difficulties; describes illness as 'atypical pneumonia;' reports two cases of disease in Canada; says outbreaks are unlikely to be related to terrorism; photo (M)

2003-03-16.title: Arab Nations Brace for an Upheaval From a War in Iraq
2003-03-16.abstract: Arabs and their leaders are waiting with varying degrees of fear and resignation for shock waves from expected United States-led war on Iraq; many in region have soured since 1991 Persian Gulf war on idea that shaking up region's established order may bring positive changes; Arab public opinion seems overwhelmingly opposed to war, athough there is little support for Saddam Hussein or his government; war poses great political risks for most Arab leaders; positions of major Mideast nations noted; photos (M)

2003-03-16.title: U.S. NAMES IRAQIS WHO WOULD FACE WAR CRIMES TRIAL
2003-03-16.abstract: US official say Bush administration has identified nine senior Iraqi officials, including Pres Saddam Hussein and sons Uday and Qusay, who would be tried for war crimes or crimes against humanity after American-led attack on Iraq; says war could be avoided if they left Iraq, replaced by leadership willing to disarm and 'open up'; Pres Bush prepares at Camp David for emergency summit meeting in Azores with British Prime Min Tony Blair and Spanish Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar; says there is little hope Hussein will disarm without use of force, radio speech; France, Russia and Germany, which have led opposition to war, call for foreign ministers to meet at Security Council to approve list of prioritized disarmament tasks and set demanding and realistic timetable; Hussein invites chief United Nations arms inspectors Hans Blix and Dr Mohamed ElBaradei to visit Iraq to discuss disarmament; Lt Gen William S Wallace, in Kuwait, says United States military will target regime, not Hussein specifically, interview; expects fighting to be concentrated in and around Baghdad (M)

2003-03-16.title: China Leader Steps Down, But Not Out Of the Picture
2003-03-16.abstract: Jiang Zemin steps down as China's president and is succeeded by Hu Jintao; Jiang, skilled diplomat, expects to continue directing foreign policy; will have to at least coordinate with official new leaders, causing diplomats and scholars to question how his official departure will affect foreign relations; he is re-elected state Central Military Commission chairman; Parliament elects his ally, Zeng Qinghong, vice president; names Wen Jiabao prime minister, succeeding Zhu Rongji; photo (M)

2003-03-16.title: Global Rallies Against War, and Some for Hussein
2003-03-16.abstract: Hundreds of demonstrations against impending war in Iraq are held around world in largely coordinated effort; crowds jam center of Madrid to protest Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar's support for war; some rallies are sparsely attended (M)

2003-03-16.title: A Figure From Iraq's Past Steps Out to Mold Its Future
2003-03-16.abstract: Adnan Pachachi, who resigned as Iraqi foreign minister when Saddam Hussein came to power and has been in talks with United States, says he is willing to take leadership role in post-invasion Iraq, interview; presents himself as patrician figure of independent and secular views that transcend ethnic and religious factions that could tear Iraq apart in war's aftermath; plans to call conference of 'independent, secular and democratic' Iraqis in London; photo (M)

2003-03-17.title: U.S. Unilateralism Worries Trade Officials
2003-03-17.abstract: Top officials at World Trade Orgn say they worry that Bush administration's go-it-alone policy is threatening international trade; fear is that American moves within WTO and toward potential war in Iraq would weaken respect for international rules and lead to serious economic consequences; Supachai Panitchpakdi, WTO's director general, says war could have devastating practical impact as world grapples with trade slowdown, rising oil prices and rising costs for transportation and insurance; photo (M)

2003-03-17.title: France Seeks Compromise To Prevent U.N. Rupture
2003-03-17.abstract: France seeks 11th-hour compromise that wil avoid rupture at United Nations over issue of American deadline for war with Iraq; Pres Jacques Chirac reiterates that France is ready to use its veto power in Security Council to block resolution that would pave way to war; insists France is not pacifist and not anti-American, but feels there is another option; this is first time Chirac has talked of fixed time for weapons inspections to end; his remarks seen as effort to avoid full-scale rupture with US; photo (M)

2003-03-17.title: Candlelight Vigils Are Held Around the World to Oppose Military Action Against Iraq
2003-03-17.abstract: Thousands of candlelight vigils are held in public squares, houses of worship and living rooms around world in subdued and widespread protests against military action against Iraq; vigils, organized largely over Internet, were conceived as form of protest that would contrast with antiwar marches and demonstrations in which tens of thousands of people have chanted, carried placards, listened to impassioned speakers and, in some cases, clashed with police; more than 50 vigils are planned at various sites in New York City alone (M)

2003-03-17.title: Realizing Dreams of Flight, Inspired by Historic Crew
2003-03-17.abstract: First article in series profiling servicemen deployed in Persian Gulf for possible war with Iraq; Lieut Dewaine Barnes is one of three black fighter pilots among 79 others on aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln; his goal is deeply rooted in history since he attributes career as aviator mostly to exploits of Tuskeegee Airmen, decorated World War II air squadron, whose members were first African-American pilots permitted to fly for US military; photos (M)

2003-03-17.title: Leading Mexican Drug Suspect Is Captured
2003-03-17.abstract: Mexican law enforcement officials report arrest of Osiel Cardenas, one of country's most wanted drug lords, after shootout in Matamoris (S)

2003-03-18.title: A Ferocious Competitor Pushes His Soldiers, and Himself, Hard
2003-03-18.abstract: Article in series Tours of Duty, which will periodically revisit Gen David H Petraeus, commanding general of 101st Airborne Division, and other service members; 101st Airborne is expected to play leading role in United States invasion of Iraq; Petraeus is responsible for 10,000 soldiers who are in Kuwait; he appears determined to carry off leading role in war that much of world has refused to endorse; says he is military commander, and not caught up in international diplomacy; says he is convinced American citizens are with him and his men; photo (M)

2003-03-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Poland: 76 Illegal Immigrants Caught
2003-03-18.abstract: Polish-German border patrols detain 76 illegal Ukrainian and Moldovan immigrants thought to be headed for Netherlands; immigrants are to be sent back to their homelands (S)

2003-03-18.title: 48-hour deadline is set; leader's sons must also go : Bush tells Saddam:Flee Iraq or face war
2003-03-18.abstract: WASHINGTON:—  President George W. Bush, in a dramatic, televised address from the White House, issued an ultimatum Monday to President Saddam Hussein: the Iraqi leader and his sons must leave the country within 48 hours or face a devastating attack by the U.S.-led forces massed in the region.

2003-03-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: Sri Lanka: Tension Before Talks Resume
2003-03-18.abstract: Sri Lanka government and Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam begin sixth round of peace talks in Japan; 20-year-old conflict between rebels fighting for autonomous Tamil state and government peaked again last week when Navy sank boat thought to be smuggling rebel weapons (S)

2003-03-18.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-03-18.abstract: HONG KONG: Financial Chief Urged To Quit; SRI LANKA: Tension Before Talks Resume;.

2003-03-19.title: U.S. Calls Decision by Hussein His 'Final Mistake'
2003-03-19.abstract: The White House said that Saddam Hussein made his "final mistake" when he rejected an ultimatum ordering him to leave Iraq or face war.

2003-03-19.title: War Imminent as Hussein Rejects Ultimatum
2003-03-19.abstract: US and Britain make final preparations for war against Iraq to topple government of Pres Saddam Hussein; US Army and Marine divisions roll toward Iraqi frontier following Pres Bush's 48-hour ultimatum to Iraq's Pres Saddam Hussein to leave country or face attack; army of 130,000 American and British soldiers, tanks and armored vehicles arc across 5,000 square miles of Kuwaiti desert; scene described; Special Operations forces reportedly begin secret missions into Iraq; commander of American and British naval forces expresses concern Iraq is preparing attacks against coalition warships; Iraqi Army's dispatch of large number of fishing vessels from coastal ports and moorings into waters where aircraft carriers, destroyers and submarines are preparing to launch aircraft and cruise missiles against targets noted; military intelligence detects preparation of missile launching sites that could be directed at naval targets; photos (M)

2003-03-19.title: Sibling Cities Issue a Plea For Decency Toward Frites
2003-03-19.abstract: Campaign to promote French-American friendliness amid disagreements over looming war with Iraq is being promoted by cities of Aix-en-Provence and sister city in Coral Gables, Fla; described; both cities issue proclamation urging both nations to recognize 'inherent decency of mankind' (M)

2003-03-19.title: Turkey Open to U.S. Using Air Space but Not Bases
2003-03-19.abstract: The government is asking Parliament to approve a resolution giving the American military the right to use Turkey's air space and air bases, but not to deploy ground troops.

2003-03-19.title: Fuel Supplies Are a Top Concern of Military Planners
2003-03-19.abstract: One of the chief worries that preoccupies military planners is that combat forces will outrun their fuel supply lines.

2003-03-20.title: European Leaders Struggle to Mend Rift With U.S.
2003-03-20.abstract: The leaders are trying to figure out how to restore unity among themselves and with the U.S., even to the point of offering to participate in Iraq's post-war reconstruction

2003-03-20.title: World Briefing | Africa: South Africa: A Plot To Kill Mandela?
2003-03-20.abstract: Twenty-three members of white extremist group Boeremag are accused of plot to assassinate former South African President Nelson Mandela; according to prosecutor Paul Frik, group, of which many are already in custody, planned to blow up Mandela's car last October; Boeremag has allegedly been involved in several bombings, including planting of nine bombs in black area of Soweto that killed one person and threatened racial stability; photo (S)

2003-03-20.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Possible Amnesty For Rebels
2003-03-20.abstract: Sergei Yastrzhembsky, aid to Russian President Vladimir V Putin, restates willingness of government to extend amnesty to Chechen rebels if they end conflict; statement, however, is simultaneous with levying formal charges against rebel leader Shamil Basayev for December bomb attack in Grozny that killed 70 people (S)

2003-03-20.title: France and Germany are left to wonder:Did they go too far?
2003-03-20.abstract: PARIS:—  In the end, beyond the maneuvering, the rhetoric, the professed convictions, there were questions in Paris and Berlin about whether France and Germany's opposition to an American-led war on Iraq got a bit out of hand.

2003-03-20.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: Journalist's Sentence Upheld
2003-03-20.abstract: Iranian court upholds 13-month sentence of reform journalist Ahmad Zeidabadi for spreading lies; court also sentences Nargess Mohammadi, wife of reformist Taqi Rahmani, to 12 months in prison; she is found guilty of jeopardizing national security during interviews she gave while her husband was in jail (S)

2003-03-21.title: Deaths in Iraq Bring Back Long History in Region
2003-03-21.abstract: Britons awoke today to grim tidings of their army's first fatalities in a distant war in Iraq.

2003-03-21.title: U.S. REPORTS TALKS URGING SURRENDER
2003-03-21.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld says allied officials have held secret talks with members of Iraq's elite Republican Guard and Special Republican Guard about defecting or surrendering; says Saddam Hussein's 'days are numbered'; says there are signs that threat of all-out American attack is prompting many Iraqi military units and their commanders to consider cease-fire or giving up; United States intelligence officials say man on videotape that was released after American missile assault on what is believed to be Hussein's bunker was probably Hussein, although no final analytical conclusion has been reached; Rumsfeld stresses that US is reaching out to Iraqi leaders and military officers with 'every conceivable mode and method'; his comments seen as latest example of aggressive psychological campaign aimed at putting pressure on Iraqi officers to give up; photo (M)

2003-03-21.title: How the Poll Was Conducted
2003-03-21.abstract: The latest New York Times/CBS News Poll is based on re-interviews with a panel of 463 adults nationwide conducted by telephone on Thursday evening. This group amounted to 46 per cent of 1,010 people who had originally been interviewed March 7 to 9 before the war in Iraq began.

2003-03-21.title: Resisting Birth Control, the Philippines Grows Crowded
2003-03-21.abstract: Government in overwhelmingly Roman Catholic Philippines has done little to curb population growth and has taken stand against modern methods of birth control; as result, population has doubled in last half-century, to 80 million, and could double again in less time than that; Philippines is often compared with Thailand; in 1970's, both countries had population of 50 million and economies of similar sizes; but Thailand has particularly active family planning program, and result is that three decades later Thai population has grown by 10 million while in Philippines there are 30 million more people; as Philippines grows more crowded, it grows ever poorer; church says it will campaign in next election against any politicians who support family planning; even some retailers are intimidated from selling condoms; photo; graph (M)

2003-03-21.title: Life in a Gas Mask: Cigar, Anyone?
2003-03-21.abstract: Reporter's Notebook describes American soldiers waiting in Scud bunkers in Kuwaiti desert, putting on gas masks as alarms go off, then taking gas masks off again; photo (M)

2003-03-22.title: Rumsfeld Says Iraq Is Collapsing, Lists 8 Objectives of War
2003-03-22.abstract: Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld says Iraqi government is starting to crumble; lays out eight broad objectives by which Bush administration will define victory; photo (M)

2003-03-22.title: Crude Test Offers Hope For Tracking Mystery Virus
2003-03-22.abstract: World Health Organization's network of scientists develops crude test to detect cases of SARS even though they have not yet precisely identified its cause; WHO expresses hope that scientists will quickly improve sensitivity of test so it can detect illness at earlier stages; map shows where cases have been reported, number of cases, and deaths (M)

2003-03-22.title: Europeans in America : Freedom fries and raw nerves
2003-03-22.abstract: From the haggling within the Western alliance over what to do about Saddam Hussein has emerged a symbol of American irritation: the freedom fry. If behind every joke lies a raw nerve, the freedom-fry protest reflects a ripple of animosity toward the French — and, by extension, other West European governments that have split with U.S. policy on Iraq. Europeans who live in the United States have an obvious, personal stake in how the backlash plays out. Among them are Patrice Higonnet and Peter Gottwald. Higonnet, 65, a professor of French history at Harvard, has dual French and American citizenship. Gottwalomat, is a fellow this year at Harvard's Weatherhead Center for International Affairs. They spoke with Joseph Rosenbloom in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

2003-03-22.title: Hamas Urges Iraqis to Make Suicide Attacks on the Invaders
2003-03-22.abstract: Leaders of Palestinian group Hamas urge Iraqis to use suicide as weapon against invading troops; Israelis continue to be on heightened state of alert; photo (M)

2003-03-22.title: World Briefing: Asia
2003-03-22.abstract: NORTH KOREA: Alarm Over War; MALAYSIA: Explosives Found; SRI LANKA: Suspected Rebels Sink Boat;.

2003-03-23.title: C.I.A. Aides Feel Pressure In Preparing Iraqi Reports
2003-03-23.abstract: Some analysts at Central Intelligence Agency say they felt pressured to make their intelligence reports on Iraq conform to Bush administration policies; debate was renewed after disclosure by Dr Mohamed ElBaradei, head of International Atomic Energy Agency, that claim that Iraq sought to buy uranium from Niger, claim cited publicly by Pres Bush, was based partly on forged documents; American intelligence officials say forged documents were not created by CIA or any other US government agency, and that they were always suspicious of them (M)

2003-03-23.title: As Many Iraqis Give Up, Some Fiercely Resist
2003-03-23.abstract: Confused scene on outskirts of Basra, largest city in southern Iraq, suggests that American and British advance, for all its initial successes, will face determined combat later on; so many Iraqi soldiers surrender so quickly that American marines hardly know what to do with them; but many others choose to stand and fight; American commanders say their forces are not likely to enter Basra, where they might face street fighting, unless Iraqi security forces and other loyalists of Saddam Hussein try to strike first; photos (M)

2003-03-23.title: Thousands Denounce War and the United States in Cities Around the World
2003-03-23.abstract: Tens of thousands of protesters around world take to streets to condemn United States-led war in Iraq; express anger at television images of Baghdad ablaze; protests in Europe draw in church leaders and politicians, schoolchildren and labor leaders; Pope John Paul II urges peace; in Spain, protesters denounce war and their government's unwavering support for military operation in Iraq; in Tokyo, demonstrators hold up photos of wounded Iraqi children in 1991 Persian Gulf war; photos (M)

2003-03-23.title: Respiratory Illness Spreads to Singapore and Vietnam
2003-03-23.abstract: In both countries the source of all new cases continues to be close contact with health care workers who themselves had close contact with earlier cases.

2003-03-23.title: Taliban Allies Kill 3 Guards Near Pakistan
2003-03-23.abstract: Taliban supporters launch two more attacks on Afghan government troops, continuing new wave of violence against American military presence in Afghanistan and American allies in Afghan government; three Afghan guards are killed in fighting 20 miles from border crossing to Pakistan; local district chief says attackers probably came across border from Pakistan (M)

2003-03-24.title: U.S. Returns 18 Guantánamo Detainees to Afghanistan
2003-03-24.abstract: It was the first large group of detainees suspected of membership in the Taliban or Al Qaeda to be released since the center opened more than a year ago.

2003-03-24.title: White House Upset Over Russian Sale of Weapons to Iraq
2003-03-24.abstract: Moscow denied that Russian companies have been selling night-vision goggles, anti-tank missiles and other hardware to Iraq.

2003-03-24.title: Rumsfeld Says Dozens of Important Targets Have Been Avoided
2003-03-24.abstract: Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld condemns Iraq's use of 'human shields' at various targets; says tactic prevents American-led air war from cutting off ability of Pres Saddam Hussein's regime to broadcast over state-run television and radio; says senior American commanders have avoided bombing as many as three dozen high-priority targets for fear of civilian casualties; map (M)

2003-03-24.title: ALLIES AND IRAQIS BATTLE ON 2 FRONTS; 20 AMERICANS DEAD OR MISSING, 50 HURT
2003-03-24.abstract: American and British ground forces suffer worst casualties so far in war against Iraq in sharpest engagements of conflict; at least 20 American soldiers die or are missing and 50 are wounded in day of ambushes, accidents and tank and artillery engagements; most of losses occur at Nasiriya, along Euphrates River about 100 miles north of Kuwaiti border where Army maintenance convoy is ambushed, resulting in death or capture of dozen soldiers; as many as 10 soldiers die during fierce battle after troop carrier is struck by rocket-propelled grenade; Pres Bush expresses grief to parents of soldiers lost in battle, but says he is confident campaign will succeed; main force of allied army races toward Bahgdad; skirts Najaf and other centers of population; Iraqi tactics show little coherent military organization, but expose potential weakness in dash by American forces to Baghdad; by skipping over cities, coalition forces appear to leave flanks and rear areas exposed to counterattacks by irregulars under command of Republican Guard; diagrams (M)

2003-03-24.title: U.S. Ties to Turkey May Face Enduring Strain, Officials Say
2003-03-24.abstract: Turkish and American officials say series of misunderstandings and miscalculations have created profound tensions that are likely to endure between two countries; political analysts cast these tensions as clear example of collateral damage from war in Iraq; photo (M)

2003-03-25.title: Kashmir Massacre May Signal the Coming of Widespread Violence
2003-03-25.abstract: Eight gunmen dressed in Indian Army uniforms enter Kashmiri village of Nadimarg, open fire and kill 24 of 52 Pandits, or Hindus, still living there; Muslims from area sit by bodies of their Hindu neighbors, wailing and cursing killers; no one has claimed responsibility for attack, but Islamic militants fighting for Kashmir's independence or accession to Pakistan have stepped up attacks on Hindus in recent years; latest attack heightens fears that with spring approaching and American attention elsewhere, violence could rise again in Jammu and Kashmir; photos; map (M)

2003-03-25.title: Weather Impedes Coalition
2003-03-25.abstract: Troops continued to move toward Baghdad, but progress was slowed by a sandstorm that brought a virtual halt to much of the war.

2003-03-25.title: From Basra, Reports of a Popular Uprising
2003-03-25.abstract: Tuesday night, there were scattered reports that Shiite civilians in Basra had begun to resist the Iraqi authorities.

2003-03-25.title: Canadian Mix of Both Jeers and Cheers for the U.S.
2003-03-25.abstract: Relations with the United States is Canada's national psychic quandary, one that produces great ambivalence and sometimes even hostility.

2003-03-25.title: British Troops Plan to Use Force in Basra
2003-03-25.abstract: The British Royal Marines said today that it had changed strategy and would now take Basra by force if necessary.

2003-03-26.title: 3rd Infantry Seizes 3 Bridges in Fiercest Fighting So Far
2003-03-26.abstract: The fight around Najaf — which lasted more than 36 hours — has considerably slowed the division's march northwards, as have the swirling winds and sand.

2003-03-26.title: Hot Disputes Over Images of P.O.W.'s Held on Each Side
2003-03-26.abstract: Treatment of 3,500 Iraqi prisoners of war becomes matter of attention after Iraqi national television shows graphic pictures of dead and captured American soldiers, prompting protests from Bush administration; allied military officials in Iraq say they take their duty to respect rights of POW's very seriously; photo (M)

2003-03-26.title: Communist Revolt Is Alive, and Active, in the Philippines
2003-03-26.abstract: World's longest-running Communist insurgency is reviving in Philippines, with attacks, ambushes and assassinations increasing sharply in last three years; photos; map; insurgency was founded in mountains near San Agustin, north of Manilla, 34 years ago and reached peak in mid-1980's as main outlet for opposition to Ferdinand Marcos regime; it withered after his fall, with many of leading figures returning to mainstream, but draws new life from persistent poverty and corruption (M)

2003-03-26.title: Displaying Solidarity, Many Iraqis Go Home
2003-03-26.abstract: About 4,330 Iraqis have returned from Jordan to Iraq to fight for Saddam Hussein, whom they once hated as oppressor; Iraqis staying in Jordan say many of those going home merely want to help their families; either way, war churns complex emotions among 380,000 Iraqis who live in Jordan; photo (M)

2003-03-26.title: Front That Brought Winds and Dust Is Due to Lift
2003-03-26.abstract: Gale-force winds and intense dust clouds hampering allied drive across southern Iraq to Baghdad is expected to clear within day; weather map (M)

2003-03-27.title: Wounded Americans Say Iraqi Soldiers Fought in Disguise
2003-03-27.abstract: Three American soldiers said they were wounded in two ambushes sprung by armed Iraqis dressed in traditional robes.

2003-03-27.title: Bush Voices Resolve, but Says the Fight May Be Long
2003-03-27.abstract: Pres Bush delivers message of resolve to American and allied military personnel at MacDill Air Force Base, but scratches portion of speech that battle plan is running ahead of schedule, as White House re-evaluates war timetable; Sec of State Colin Powell is direct in answering criticism that American force is not large enough to handle its task, saying public should not be influenced by one day's ups and downs; other adminstration officials respond to similar queries about current strength of force; photos (M)

2003-03-27.title: World Briefing: Africa
2003-03-27.abstract: ZAMBIA: Rains Destroy Homes And Crops; LIBERIA: Rebels Close In On Capital;.

2003-03-27.title: Food Arrives, but Water Supplies Cause Worry
2003-03-27.abstract: First food convoy arrives at Iraqi border town of Safwan; reports that residents of Basra are drinking water from putrid rivers, raise fear of outbreak of disease; United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan voices concern about humanitarian crisis that could spiral out of control; photo (M)

2003-03-27.title: Fight Far From Over in Kifl
2003-03-27.abstract: Army forces seized a toehold in this village beginning late Monday night. Seventy-two hours later the division has a foothold, but the fight continues.

2003-03-28.title: Hong Kong closes its schools to curb outbreak
2003-03-28.abstract: HONG KONG:—  Faced with an increased infection rate of a mysterious killer pneumonia, Hong Kong closed schools for 1 million students Thursday and imposed a quarantine on more than a thousand residents.

2003-03-28.title: Explosions Strike Baghdad Again; 50 Are Reported Dead
2003-03-28.abstract: Baghdad was hit early today by explosions after some of the most powerful bombardments of the Iraqi capital since the start of the war 10 days ago.

2003-03-28.title: Hans Blix Expected to Leave His Inspection Post in June
2003-03-28.abstract: Hans Blix, chief weapons inspector, says he expects to leave his post when his contract expires in June; his measured reports on Iraq's limited and grudging cooperation helped scuttle chances of direct UN approval of current war there (S)

2003-03-28.title: Supply Lines Under Attack and Action in the North
2003-03-28.abstract: As sandstorms died down and the skies over Iraq finally cleared, allied commanders faced a choice between resuming the advance toward Baghdad or securing their supply lines. American aircraft carried out heavy bombing raids against Baghdad, while in the north, the first major movements brought Kurdish fighters into the battle. From inside Baghdad, Iraq's defense minister pledged that the city would be defended street by street.

2003-03-28.title: C.I.A. Warned Pentagon Of Guerrilla Tactics
2003-03-28.abstract: Intelligence analysts at Central Intelligence Agency, Defense Intelligence Agency and National Ground Intelligence Center warned senior Pentagon officials before war in Iraq began that paramilitary units loyal to Saddam Hussein would fight back and could pose significant threat but did not make it dominant theme, which perhaps explains why commanders in field were caught by surprise (M)

2003-03-29.title: Excerpts From Bush's Speech
2003-03-29.abstract: Following are excerpts from an address by President Bush to veterans groups yesterday at the White House, as recorded by The New York Times:.

2003-03-29.title: Britain Asks, 'Who Wants to Be a Prisoner?'
2003-03-29.abstract: Britons are riveted by raucous and often comic criminal trial of Charles Ingram, British Army major, and his wife, Diana, and Tecwen Whittock, who are accused of cheating on television quiz show Who Wants to be a Millionaire?, signaling correct answers by means of strategic coughing; Chris Tarrant, quiz show's host, testifies for prosecution; photos (M)

2003-03-29.title: Turkish Jet Is Hijacked and Flown to Athens
2003-03-29.abstract: Turkish Airlines flight on its way to Ankara from Istanbul is hijacked and forced to fly to Athens, where it sits on tarmac for three hours before the more than 190 passengers on board are released; Turkish officials say there was just one hijacker, 22-year-old Turkish man identified as Ozgur Gencarslan, and he does not have known ties to any terrorist group; passengers say he appeared to be emotionally unstable (M)

2003-03-29.title: World's airports step up measures to fight virus
2003-03-29.abstract: HONG KONG:—  Battling to contain a mysterious and fast-spreading pneumonia that has killed more than 50 people, Hong Kong on Friday issued an urgent appeal for all persons who had traveled aboard two flights that carried infected passengers to report to health authorities immediately.

2003-03-29.title: WHITE HOUSE SAYS WAR IS 'ON TRACK'; SHOW OF SUPPORT
2003-03-29.abstract: Bush administration works to firm up support for war, accusing Saddam Hussein of operating death squad, warning Syria and Iran agains interfering with allied opeations and mounting concerted effort to counter impression that fighting had gone worse than expected in first nine days; admininstration officials express confidence in strength and flexibililty of their military plan, and say current movement of 100,000 additional troops to region was part of plan all along; Gen Richad B Myers, chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, sats plan is brilliant; Bush photo (L)

2003-03-30.title: Seeing Face Of Real War In the Devil Of Details
2003-03-30.abstract: Troops with 101st Airborne Division take Iraqi prisoners to interrogation area near Najaf; ordinary confusions of war persist, including misplaced prisoners and missing gear; troops have encircled Najaf, but have not yet entered city as waves of Iraqi paramilitary fighters in town carry out waves of attacks that have managed to keep Americans at bay (M)

2003-03-30.title: U.S. Finds Buried Bodies of 4 G.I.'s
2003-03-30.abstract: Military officials said they believed that the four were executed by Iraqi paramilitary forces after they were seized in an ambush last Sunday.

2003-03-30.title: U.S. Convoy Is Ambushed; 2 Americans Are Killed
2003-03-30.abstract: Two American soldiers are killed and one is wounded when convoy of Special Forces unit is attacked in southwestern Afghanistan; names of dead are being withheld pending notification of families; Americans have suffered 18 casualties in Afghanistan since Oct 2001; local police chief Muhammad Wali Khan blames Taliban or Al Qaeda for attack (M)

2003-03-30.title: Syrian Party Watches Iraq With Unease and Ponders Its Own Fate
2003-03-30.abstract: Questions about what comes next in Iraq generate sharp sense of dread in neighboring Syria, controlled since 1963 by rival branch of same Baath Party that grips Iraq; officials dismiss notion of any possible effect of war or any unease that their country might come under US attack, even as Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld issues harsh warning about allowing military equipment to flow into Iraq, charge which Syria quickly denies; although Pres Bashar al-Assad came into office voicing vision of country with less official control, change has not come and reform appears distant (M)

2003-03-30.title: Commentary From Around the World Shows Division Still Deep
2003-03-30.abstract: The following excerpts of editorial commentary from around the world are by turns damning and supportive of the war in Iraq.

2003-03-31.title: Rumsfeld Defends War Planning
2003-03-31.abstract: The defense secretary said that he and his commanders had not underestimated the number of ground troops needed.

2003-03-31.title: INFANTRY ATTACKS BAGHDAD DEFENSE WITH FIRST PROBES
2003-03-31.abstract: American Marine and Army infantry units arrayed along broad front south of Baghdad make first probing attacks against Republican Guard division at Karbala, 65 miles from capital; this follows week of heavy aerial bombardment on Iraqi forces that guard approaches to Saddam Hussein's stronghold; in Baghdad, another round of airstrikes begins; bombing continues on city's southern outskirts, where at last two Republican Guard divisions are deployed; probing attacks are intended to keep pressure on Hussein and to re-establish allied momentum that has been stalled in recent days by fierce attacks on allied supply lines; Gen Tommy R Franks, American commander in region, disputes charge that allied commanders misjudged willingness of Hussein's loyalists to resist and underestimated size of armed force needed to sudue them; defends his war plan, as do Gen Richard B Myers, chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff, and Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld; despite Franks's confidence, situation in Iraq appears tense; war continues to unfold as far more complex tapestry than expected, with dispersed engagements, political and psychological warfare against Hussein's government, mobilization to deliver aid to south, frenetic diplomacy and shifting explanations at home about war's duration and cost; photos; map (L)

2003-03-31.title: Officer Says Iraqis Are Skeptical of U.S. Supporting Revolt
2003-03-31.abstract: The U.S., through its past acts, is largely to blame for the failure of Iraq's Shiite majority to rise in revolt against Saddam Hussein, a senior military commander said.

2003-03-31.title: U.S. Fights Closer to Baghdad; Skirmishes in South
2003-03-31.abstract: Allied forces leading the drive north to Baghdad battled their way into a town 50 miles south of the capital.

2003-03-31.title: A Chinese Hotel, Full of Proud American Parents
2003-03-31.abstract: Luxury hotel in Guangzhou, China, is where Americans spend their first days with their newly adopted Chinese children; hotel is less than block from United States Consulate, where paperwork for most adopted Chinese children heading to US must be processed; adoption agencies often book large portions of floor at hotel for groups of American families traveling together to adopt in China; Americans typically get to China after waiting more than year to adopt child; State Dept says 4,500 adopted Chinese children were issued visas to enter US in 2001; they are almost entirely girls; map (M)
